nativeRange.habitat: Shallow lagoon and seaward reefs in areas of mixed coral, rock, or sand, typically at depths of 2–30 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding primarily on benthic algae and detritus in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to offer dried marine seaweed (nori) on a clip at least three times per week, alongside spirulina-based frozen and prepared foods. Meaty foods such as mysis are tolerated occasionally, but a vegetable-dominant diet is widely considered important for long-term coloration and immune health.

avoidWith: fish of similar body shape and color

imageCandidates.notes: Ian Shaw (Reef Life Survey), 1200x800. Sharp full-body lateral juvenile showing the diagnostic Eibli-mimic look: grey body with orange vertical lines, yellow-orange belly, black caudal fin with blue margins, orange eye-ring. Small watermark bottom-right.

iNaturalist observer (via Wikimedia Commons), 2048x1386. Adult phase for variety: brown body with retained orange highlights near eye and pectoral, pale lyre-shaped tail. Slightly soft but correctly ID'd and well-lit.
Iran V. Shaw, 1024x683. Sharp, clean lateral juvenile; same classic coloration/pattern as the recommended pick (mildly duplicative but crisp and correctly ID'd).

summary: The Mimic Eibli Tang is an Indian Ocean surgeonfish (Acanthurus tristis) whose juveniles closely resemble the Eibli Angelfish (Centropyge eibli). Generally considered reef-safe and a useful algae grazer, it tends to be highly aggressive toward other tangs and surgeonfish.
careNotes:
The Mimic Eibli Tang (Acanthurus tristis) is an Indian Ocean surgeonfish best known for its juvenile mimicry of the Eibli Angelfish (Centropyge eibli) — a trait thought to afford some predator protection on the reef. Juveniles display blue, white, and yellow coloration with striking orange vertical lines and a yellow-to-orange belly that darkens toward a black caudal region. Adults gradually shift toward more uniform brown tones with retained orange highlights near the eye and pectoral fins.

Like all Acanthurus surgeonfish, this species possesses a sharp, blade-like caudal-peduncle spine (scalpel) capable of inflicting cuts on handlers and tankmates — caution is advised when netting or moving the fish. The Mimic Eibli Tang is widely considered highly aggressive toward conspecifics and other tangs or surgeonfish, particularly those of similar body shape, but tends to be peaceful with unrelated fish. Keeping only one specimen is generally recommended unless the aquarium is exceptionally large.

A well-established reef aquarium with ample live rock for grazing, open swimming space, and strong, well-oxygenated flow suits this species well. Where natural algae growth is insufficient, regular nori or spirulina-based supplementation helps maintain the fish's condition and reduce aggression. Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) — a condition that causes pitting and discoloration along the head and sensory canals — is reported to be a risk for this species, as with most tangs, when kept in poor water quality or on a low-algae diet.
